---
title: "Example Workflow"
author: Gang Chen
date: 5/2/2026
---

Use relative paths and a clear folder structure. A typical research project can use this structure:

``` text
project-name/
|-- data/
|   |-- raw/              # Original, immutable data; do not edit directly
|   |-- interim/          # Intermediate data generated during processing
|   `-- processed/        # Cleaned or transformed datasets
|-- code/
|   |-- notebooks/        # Exploratory analysis and prototyping
|   |-- scripts/          # Reusable scripts for data processing and analysis
|   |-- src/              # Core reusable project code
|   `-- tests/            # Unit tests and validation checks
|-- output/
|   |-- figures/          # Generated plots, charts, and images
|   |-- tables/           # Generated tables and result summaries
|   `-- models/           # Trained models or serialized outputs
|-- reports/
|   |-- drafts/           # Working versions of reports or papers
|   |-- final/            # Finalized reports
|   `-- presentations/    # Slides and presentation materials
|-- docs/
|   |-- plan.md           # Project goals, milestones, timeline, and tasks
|   |-- context.md        # Background, motivation, assumptions, and scope
|   `-- references.md     # Key references and supporting materials
|-- README.md             # Project overview, setup, and usage instructions
|-- .gitignore            # Exclude confidential data, credentials, and large raw files
`-- LICENSE               # Licensing terms
```

Keep raw data separate from cleaned data. Record cleaning decisions. Use scripts or Quarto documents that can be rerun from start to finish.

### Development Cycle

Analysis should be incremental. Run the code after each major change and check:

-   data dimensions and variable names,
-   missing values and outliers,
-   joins and merges,
-   descriptive statistics,
-   sensitivity to alternative specifications.

Commit the project after a meaningful unit of work is complete and the output has been reviewed.
